planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist potting mix or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun after frost; king's mantle is one of the most shade-tolerant of the flowering tropical shrubs and blooms reliably in conditions where full-sun shrubs would fail to flower — it is an excellent choice for shaded courtyards and partially shaded garden borders.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; king's mantle (bush clock vine, royal purple thunbergia) is a West African evergreen shrub of the family Acanthaceae that produces large, trumpet-shaped, deep-purple to violet flowers with pale-yellow to white throats continuously from spring through late fall — the flowers are among the richest and most deeply saturated purple-blue of any flowering shrub available for the Gulf Coast and tropical garden. The dark, metallic, deep-purple flower color against the glossy, dark-green, oval leaves creates a striking combination that remains outstanding throughout the long blooming season. Unlike most purple-flowering tropical plants that demand full sun, king's mantle blooms acceptably in partial shade, making it particularly valuable for lightly shaded garden areas where comparable purple-flowered plants would fail. It grows as a mounding, arching shrub with woody lower stems and softer, freely-branching upper growth that flowers on new wood. In zone 9 it may freeze back but regrows reliably from the roots in spring. Excellent as a specimen, informal hedge, or color accent in the partial-shade border.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to poor soil in partial shade in spring; dicliptera is drought-tolerant once established and actually blooms better in poor, dry soil than in rich, fertile conditions — avoid heavy fertilization which produces rank foliage at the expense of flowers.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in partial shade; Uruguayan firecracker plant (hummingbird plant) is a South American perennial of the family Acanthaceae that stands out from other hummingbird plants by its distinctive silvery-gray, softly hairy foliage — the felt-like, silver-green leaves create a refined, cool-toned foliage effect that combines beautifully with the bright, tubular, orange-red flowers borne in clusters at the stem tips from late spring through fall. The silver foliage and orange-red flowers together create a sophisticated color combination that is warm, vivid, and unusual. It is notably drought- and heat-tolerant, shade-tolerant, and hardy to approximately 15°F in zone 8 (freezing back and regrowing reliably from the root). The clusters of bright-orange tubular flowers are irresistible to hummingbirds and produce nectar continuously through the warm season. It spreads moderately by underground stolons to form manageable colonies that make it effective as a groundcover on slopes and dry, shaded areas. An outstanding plant for hot, dry, partially shaded conditions in zone 8–11 where many flowering perennials fail.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist potting mix or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist to average soil in partial shade after frost; red ruellia is one of the better shade-tolerant flowering perennials for zone 8–10, maintaining flower production in quite dim conditions where most red-flowered tropical plants would fail to bloom.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, moist to average soil in partial shade; red ruellia is a Brazilian tropical perennial of the family Acanthaceae that produces bright, clear-red, tubular, flared, funnel-shaped flowers continuously from spring through frost in partial-shade conditions where many red-flowering tropicals would cease blooming — making it particularly valuable as a continuously-flowering red accent plant for shaded gardens. The flowers are individually short-lived (opening for a single day) but produced in continuous succession on the upright stems, creating an ongoing display. The dark-green, lance-shaped leaves provide attractive foliage coverage between flowering periods. Distinguished from the weedy Mexican petunia (Ruellia simplex, previously R. brittoniana) by its non-invasive growth habit, larger flowers, and non-spreading by seed — red ruellia does not naturalize in natural habitats and is safe to plant in ecologically sensitive areas. Excellent as a hummingbird plant, the tubular red flowers attract hummingbirds reliably. In zone 8–9 it freezes back but regrows from roots in spring.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist potting mix or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile, humus-rich soil in partial shade after frost; Brazilian yellow sage blooms most heavily in fall and winter in frost-free climates and performs best in consistent moisture with partial-shade protection from intense afternoon sun, which causes leaf scorch.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, average to fertile, humus-rich soil in partial shade; Brazilian yellow sage (yellow jacobinia, golden justicia, golden plume) is a Central American tropical shrub of the family Acanthaceae that produces dense, upright, cone-shaped spikes of bright-yellow, tubular, two-lipped flowers in the fall and winter months — at a time when yellow-flowering shade plants are exceptionally rare. The golden-yellow flower spikes, clustered densely at the tips of the arching stems, provide a vivid autumn-to-winter color display in the warm-climate garden that is nearly unmatched among partial-shade flowering shrubs. The large, soft, oval, velvety-hairy leaves are attractive and provide bold foliage texture in the shade garden. Like other shade-tolerant members of the Acanthaceae, it performs remarkably well in the dappled light of open tree canopies — one of few large-growing shade-tolerant shrubs that produce ornamental spikes of flowers rather than solitary tubular flowers. In zone 9 it freezes to the ground but regrows vigorously in spring. Cut back after flowering to encourage compact regrowth.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile, humus-rich soil in partial shade after frost; shooting star performs best in bright indirect light or light-dappled shade — deep shade diminishes its leaf patterning, while intense direct sun bleaches and scorches the variegated leaves.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile, humus-rich soil in partial shade; shooting star is a Pacific Island tropical shrub of the family Acanthaceae grown primarily for its striking foliage — the oval to lance-shaped leaves are broadly variegated in an irregular blotch pattern of deep purple, green, cream, and ivory-white, creating a bold, multicolored effect that is ornamental year-round and serves a similar role to coleus in the shaded tropical garden. Periodically through the year, the plant produces loose clusters of small, tubular, white flowers with purple-spotted throats, adding to the display. The cultivated forms include purple-leaved and cream-variegated variants, all under the variable species classification. It requires genuine tropical warmth (zone 10–12) and resents cold temperatures — even brief chilling below 50°F can cause leaf discoloration and drop. Most effective in the warm, bright-shade microclimate of an established tropical garden, where its bold variegated foliage provides a strong mid-border accent. Excellent for shaded container plantings and outdoor tropical rooms in zone 10–12.
